# Locality Guide: Louisiana — French (and Spanish) Colonial Period (1699–1803)

A note on scope: the **1700s** straddle two colonial regimes — **French (1699–1762)** and **Spanish (1762–1800)** — plus a brief French interlude before the Louisiana Purchase (1803). Both are covered here because families appear across both regimes and many record-keeping institutions were continuous.

---

## Jurisdiction overview

Louisiana was founded as a French colony in 1699; New Orleans was established in 1718. France secretly ceded the territory to Spain in 1762 (Treaty of Fontainebleau), Spain administered it until 1800, and the U.S. acquired it in 1803. Internally, the colony was organized into military **posts** and ecclesiastical **parishes** — not U.S.-style counties, which didn't appear until after statehood in 1812.

The Roman Catholic Church was the **only legally recognized church** in both French and Spanish periods. The *Code Noir* (Louisiana edition, 1724) required Catholic baptism of all enslaved persons, which means church registers capture a far broader slice of the population than in most colonial settings.

---

## Available record types

### Church records — **the primary vital records substitute**

Civil registration never existed in colonial Louisiana. Church registers are what you search instead of birth/marriage/death certificates.

- **Earliest records:** St. Louis Cathedral (New Orleans) registers survive from **1720** — the oldest continuous Catholic registers in what is now the U.S.
- **What exists:** Baptisms, marriages, burials, confirmations, and slave registers (listing enslaved persons by name, often with age and origin).
- **Language:** French (most of the 1700s); some Spanish entries in the late 1700s.
- **Best starting point:** **FamilySearch, "Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925"** (Collection ID 1612905) — ~988,000 records, **indexed + images, free**. This is the single most accessible entry point.
- **Original registers:** Held at the **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives** (by appointment). Some rural post registers (Natchitoches, Opelousas, Attakapas, Pointe Coupée) are not fully digitized and have gaps.
- **Record losses:** The 1788 and 1794 New Orleans fires destroyed some registers. Rural coverage is patchier than New Orleans.

---

### Superior Council / Cabildo records (civil and judicial)

The *Conseil Supérieur* (French period) and *Cabildo* (Spanish period) handled courts, contracts, estate inventories, guardianships, and land concessions. These are the colonial-period equivalent of probate and court records.

- **Types:** Estate inventories, successions, guardianships, land concessions, contracts, criminal/civil suits, slave manumissions, freedom papers.
- **Date range:** ca. 1720s–1803.
- **Where held:** **Louisiana Historical Center** (Old U.S. Mint, 400 Esplanade Ave., New Orleans) — partially digitized on the Louisiana Digital Library; much is **physical only**.
- **Access level:** Browse-only images (partial); physical only for the rest.

---

### Notarial records — often more informative than church registers

The New Orleans Notarial Archives is the oldest continuously operating notarial office in North America, with records from the **1730s onward**.

- **Types:** Marriage contracts (*contrats de mariage*), wills, property sales, slave sales and manumissions, apprenticeship contracts, powers of attorney.
- **Why critical:** Under French/Spanish civil law, propertied families recorded marriages before a notary. These contracts routinely name both sets of parents, making them a functional birth-record substitute.
- **Where held:** **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center**, 1340 Poydras St., Suite 300 — open to the public with on-site finding aids. Some volumes are on **FamilySearch microfilm** (search the FamilySearch Catalog under "Louisiana — Notarial Records").
- **Access level:** Physical only (most); microfilm at FHL for some volumes.

---

### Colonial census records (*dénombrements*)

The French colonial government conducted periodic enumerations: **1699, 1708, 1721, 1724, 1726, 1731–32**, and others. These list heads of household, family members, and enslaved persons — often with ages, occupations, and landholdings.

- **Where held:**
  - **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)**, Aix-en-Provence, France — the definitive repository. Free online portal at anom.archivesnationales.culture.gouv.fr (in French).
  - **Louisiana Historical Center** — some transcripts/microfilm.
  - Charles Maduell's *Census Tables for the French Colony of Louisiana* (1972) transcribes several enumerations and is available in major genealogical libraries.
- **Access level:** Physical only (ANOM originals); microfilm at some FHL branches; published transcriptions in print.

---

### Land records

- **System:** French long-lot *concession* system (not rectangular survey) — farms fronting on waterways.
- **After the Purchase:** All colonial-era land grant holders had to file U.S. claims. The **American State Papers: Public Lands** (free online at HathiTrust and Memory of Congress) list confirmed French and Spanish grants with original grant date and grantee — a direct link between a colonial ancestor and a landholding.
- **Where held:** Louisiana State Land Office (Baton Rouge) for originals; ANOM for French concession records; Archivo General de Indias (Seville) for Spanish grants.
- **Access level:** Indexed + images for American State Papers (online free); physical only for originals.

---

### Probate records

The U.S.-style probate system didn't exist in colonial Louisiana; use Superior Council estate inventories instead (above). For records overlapping the end of the colonial period:

- **FamilySearch, "Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984"** (Collection ID 2078341) — ~457,000 records, **indexed + images, free**. The 1756 start captures some late-colonial and territorial material.

---

### Military records

Relevant conflicts include the Seven Years' War / French and Indian War (1754–1763) and the American Revolution (Spain fought Britain in Louisiana, 1779–1783).

- **French period:** Troop lists and officer records in **ANOM** (France) — *Dépôt des Fortifications des Colonies* and related series.
- **Spanish period:** **Archivo General de Indias** (Seville) — free partial access via PARES (pares.mcu.es).
- **Access level:** Mostly physical only or browse-only images via PARES; no comprehensive indexed online database for this period.

---

### Immigration records

There was no formal immigration processing. Evidence of arrival comes from:
- **Ship manifests in ANOM** for colonists, soldiers, and *engagés* (indentured workers).
- **Acadian exile records (arriving 1764–1785):** Steve White's *Dictionnaire généalogique des familles acadiennes* is the standard reference; also the Acadian Museum (Erath, LA) and Université de Moncton.
- **German Coast settlers (ca. 1720–1740):** Transcribed arrival lists have been published.
- **Enslaved Africans:** The *Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database* (slavevoyages.org, free, indexed) traces voyages; individual names are rarely recorded, but port of origin and ship are traceable.

---

### Newspapers

None were published in the French colonial period. The *Moniteur de la Louisiane* (1794) is the earliest Louisiana newspaper and covers only the very end of the Spanish period. LSU Libraries and Chronicling America hold microfilm.

---

## Repository guide

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Church Records 1718–1925; Probate Records 1756–1984 | Free, indexed + images |
| **ANOM**, Aix-en-Provence, France | French colonial admin records, census rolls, troop lists, ship records | Free portal (French); physical for full access |
| **PARES / Archivo Gral. de Indias**, Seville | Spanish colonial Louisiana records post-1762 | Free partial online |
| **Louisiana Digital Library** | Partial Superior Council / Cabildo records | Free, browse-only |
| **slavevoyages.org** | Trans-Atlantic slave trade voyage data | Free, indexed |
| **HathiTrust / Memory of Congress** | American State Papers: Public Lands | Free, indexed |
| **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives** | Original sacramental registers from 1720 | By appointment |
| **Louisiana Historical Center** (New Orleans) | Superior Council/Cabildo records; colonial census transcripts | Public |
| **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** | Notarial acts from 1730s | Public |
| **Louisiana State Land Office**, Baton Rouge | Colonial and territorial land grants and plats | In-person / mail |
| **Family History Library**, Salt Lake City | Microfilm of church registers, notarial records | In-person or FHL loan |

---

## Records NOT online

These exist and matter — don't assume they're gone just because they're not in an online database:

- Superior Council / Cabildo records in full (mostly physical-only at the Louisiana Historical Center)
- Notarial acts at the New Orleans Notarial Archives (no searchable online database)
- ANOM census rolls and correspondence in their entirety
- Archivo General de Indias Louisiana records beyond PARES digitization
- Rural post registers for Natchitoches, Opelousas, Attakapas, Pointe Coupée (incomplete on FamilySearch)

---

## Known record losses

- **New Orleans fires of 1788 and 1794** destroyed some church and civil records; notarial records partially survived (notaries kept records in brick vaults).
- **Rural post registers** have significant gaps — some were never collected or microfilmed.
- **Climate damage** (heat, humidity, flooding) has degraded unhoused records over the centuries.
- **Substitutes for lost records:** Notarial marriage contracts for missing church marriage entries; Superior Council estate inventories for missing probate; census rolls to place a family in time and location.

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with FamilySearch's church records collection** — it's indexed, free, and covers the most critical record type for this period.
2. **Records are in French** (and some Spanish). Plan for translation; 18th-century French handwriting can be challenging.
3. **Check notarial marriage contracts.** They commonly name both sets of parents and predate civil registration by over a century.
4. **The ANOM is essential for immigrants and soldiers.** If an ancestor arrived from France or Canada, or served in the colonial troops, records exist there that are nowhere else.
5. **Acadian ancestry:** White's *Dictionnaire* is the standard reference; most Acadian families are extremely well-documented.
6. **Enslaved ancestors:** Church baptismal registers list enslaved persons by name (Code Noir requirement). Estate inventories name them and sometimes give age and origin.
7. **American State Papers confirm colonial land ownership** — free online, and often the only English-language source connecting a colonial ancestor to a specific landholding.
8. **Published compiled genealogies:** Louisiana colonial genealogy has unusually rich published resources — Winston De Ville's *Louisiana Colonials*, Donald Hébert's *Southwest Louisiana Records*, and Maduell's census transcriptions are worth locating before diving into original records.

---

## Key takeaways

**The most important records for this region and period — and where to find them:**

### The coal mining difference
This region has records you won't find elsewhere:
- **Pennsylvania Mine Inspector Annual Reports (1870+)** — published annually, list every fatal mine accident with name, age, and nationality. Many volumes are free on Google Books/HathiTrust. They predate death certificates by decades.
- **Company employment records** — survive fragmentarily at the PA State Archives and Historical Society of Schuylkill County. Don't expect a complete run; what exists is worth checking.
- **UMWA records (post-1890)** — at Penn State's Eberly Special Collections.

### The big gap: no civil vital records before 1893
Pennsylvania didn't require civil registration until 1893, and even then it was poorly enforced in mining towns until 1906. For births, marriages, and deaths before those dates, **ethnic church registers are your primary source** — which means you need to identify the right parish before you can search it.

### Most useful online collections (FamilySearch, free)
| Collection | Records |
|---|---|
| Pennsylvania Death Certificates 1906–1968 | 4.2M, indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania Probate Records 1683–1994 | 893K, indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania Church & Town Records 1708–1985 | 1.24M, indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania County Marriages 1885–1950 | 524K, indexed + images |

### Naturalization records are underused
Schuylkill County naturalizations (1840s–1906) are at the **PA State Archives** and often name the immigrant's country — sometimes the specific village. This is frequently the record that breaks open an immigrant origin question.

### The *Miners' Journal* (Pottsville, est. 1825)
This paper is a de facto vital records substitute for the pre-1893 gap. Obituaries routinely name nativity, relatives, and years in the U.S. Microfilm at the Historical Society of Schuylkill County; partial digitization on Chronicling America (free).

# Locality Guide: Massachusetts — Early Colonial / Puritan Settlements (1620–1700)

## Jurisdiction overview

The early 1600s involve **two separate colonies** with distinct record systems:

- **Plymouth Colony** (1620–1691) — governed by the General Court of Plymouth; merged into Massachusetts Bay by the 1691 charter
- **Massachusetts Bay Colony** (1628/1630–1691) — governed by the General Court of Massachusetts Bay; Boston as administrative center

**County system:** There were no counties before **1643**, when the Bay Colony created four: **Suffolk** (Boston area), **Essex** (Salem/Ipswich), **Middlesex** (Cambridge/Concord), and **Old Norfolk** (which included territory now in southern New Hampshire). Before 1643, all civil and judicial records were created at the **colony level**.

**Population:** ~900 settlers (1630) → ~20,000 by end of the Great Migration (1643) → ~55,000–60,000 by 1675. King Philip's War (1675–1676) caused significant population displacement and destroyed records in several towns.

**Religion:** Congregational churches were the sole official institution. No Baptist, Quaker, or Anglican congregations were legally established in the Bay Colony before 1700.

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (town registration) — Indexed + images
- **Start date: 1639.** Colony law required each town clerk to record births, marriages, and deaths. This makes Massachusetts one of the earliest civil registration jurisdictions in North America.
- **Key resource:** The **Tanner–Corbin "Vital Records to 1850" series** abstracts town clerk books for 200+ towns. This is your primary index.
- **FamilySearch:** *Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890* — ~3.4 million indexed records with images → [collection/2061550](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2061550)
- **Gaps:** Pre-1639 events have no civil record (use church records). King Philip's War burned records for Deerfield, Northampton, Brookfield, Medfield, and others. Some towns complied inconsistently before the 1650s–1660s.

### Church records (Congregational) — Indexed + images / Physical
- The established church kept **membership admissions, baptisms, marriages, and discipline records**. For events before 1639, these are your *only* source.
- Many early records are **published** (Colonial Society of Massachusetts series); the rest are at the **Congregational Library & Archives** (Boston) or on FamilySearch.
- **FamilySearch:** *Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920* — ~987,000 records → [collection/1921657](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1921657)

### Colony and court records — Free online (published) / Physical (originals)
- General Court minutes and acts survive from **1628** (Bay) and **1620** (Plymouth) and have been **fully published**:
  - *Records of the Governor and Company of the Massachusetts Bay* (Shurtleff, 5 vols., 1853–1854)
  - *Records of the Colony of New Plymouth* (Shurtleff & Pulsifer, 12 vols., 1855–1861)
  - *Winthrop Papers* (Mass. Historical Society, 6 vols.)
- All are freely available on **HathiTrust** and the **Internet Archive**.
- Originals at the **Massachusetts Archives** (Boston).

### Probate records — Indexed + images
- Begin in **1635–1636** for Suffolk; from 1643 for other counties.
- Wills, administrations, inventories — highly useful for establishing family relationships when vital records are sparse.
- **FamilySearch:** *Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991* — ~567,000 records → [collection/1678901](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1678901)

### Land records — Varies (browse-only to physical)
- **Metes and bounds** system. Deeds recorded at county Registries of Deeds from **1643**; pre-1643 land grants appear in colony-level records.
- **Proprietors' records** — each founding town kept separate books recording original land divisions. These are often the earliest records for a town and are partially published, partially physical-only at town clerks and NEHGS.
- County deed books are partially online (Ancestry, FamilySearch); early volumes vary from browse-only to physical-only.

### Tax records — Physical / Published transcriptions
- Colony "rates" survive from the **1630s** at the Massachusetts Archives.
- The **1643 military assessment** is effectively the closest thing to a colony-wide census for adult men — it lists men by town.
- Tax lists substitute for any missing census-type records and confirm a person's presence in a specific town in a given year.

### Military records — Physical / Published transcriptions
- Relevant conflicts: **Pequot War (1636–1638)** and **King Philip's War (1675–1676)**
- Muster rolls, pay lists, and casualty lists — held at the Massachusetts Archives and Massachusetts Historical Society
- Several lists published in Massachusetts Historical Society *Collections* (available on HathiTrust)

### Census records — N/A (no census before 1790)
- No federal or colonial census survives. Use **tax lists**, **church membership rolls**, and the **1643 military assessment** as substitutes.

### Immigration / passenger records
- **The Great Migration Study** (NEHGS, Robert Charles Anderson) has documented every known settler arriving in New England **1620–1643**. Each entry cites all known sources, documents English origins when identified, and lists children and associates. This is the indispensable starting point for Puritan-era immigrant ancestors.
- Access: **AmericanAncestors.org** (NEHGS subscription) or the published volumes in major research libraries.
- English departure records: **The National Archives** (Kew, UK); some published transcriptions on Ancestry.

### Cemetery records — Indexed + images (inscriptions) / Physical (sexton)
- Major early burial grounds: King's Chapel Burying Ground (Boston, 1630), Granary Burying Ground (Boston, 1660), Copp's Hill (Boston, 1659), plus grounds in Salem, Plymouth, Cambridge, and most early towns.
- Gravestones are sparse before ~1660–1680 (Puritan practice); surviving inscriptions widely transcribed on **FindAGrave** and **BillionGraves**.
- No formal sexton records in this period — town vital records and church records are better sources.

### Newspapers — Not applicable before 1700
- No newspapers existed in Massachusetts before 1690. The first continuous paper (*Boston News-Letter*) began in 1704. Newspapers are not a useful source for this period.

---

## Repository guide

### Online

| Repository | What's there | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Town Vital Records; Church Records; Probate Records | Free |
| **Ancestry** (ancestry.com) | Vital records, church records, probate, land records, military | Subscription |
| **AmericanAncestors.org** (NEHGS) | Great Migration database; NEHGS manuscript collections | Subscription |
| **HathiTrust / Internet Archive** | Published colony records (Shurtleff, Pulsifer); Tanner–Corbin vital records series; local histories | Free |
| **Mass. Digital Commonwealth** (digitalcommonwealth.org) | Digitized manuscripts, maps, and photographs from MA repositories | Free |

### Physical

| Repository | Holdings |
|---|---|
| **Massachusetts Archives** (Boston) | Original colony court records, General Court acts, military records, deposited town records |
| **NEHGS** (Boston) | Largest genealogical library in New England; manuscripts; Great Migration files |
| **Massachusetts Historical Society** (Boston) | Winthrop Papers, Mather papers, manuscript diaries, military records |
| **Congregational Library & Archives** (Boston) | Original Congregational church record books; some of the earliest church registers in America |
| **County Registries of Deeds** | Original deed books from 1643+ (one registry per county) |
| **Pilgrim Hall Museum / Pilgrim Society** (Plymouth) | Plymouth Colony manuscripts; Mayflower passenger records |
| **Phillips Library / Peabody Essex Museum** (Salem) | Salem-area manuscripts; early Essex County records |

---

## Records NOT online

- Original **town proprietors' records** — mostly at town clerks or local historical societies; search via NEHGS catalog
- **Colony tax rates (1630s–1690s)** — Massachusetts Archives; not searchable online
- Early Congregational **discipline records** — Congregational Library & Archives; partially on FamilySearch
- **King Philip's War muster rolls** — Massachusetts Archives
- Manuscript **county court papers** (below General Court level) — Massachusetts Archives; mostly not digitized

---

## Known record losses

| Loss | What was destroyed | Substitute |
|------|-------------------|------------|
| **King Philip's War (1675–76)** | Town records for Deerfield, Northampton, Brookfield, Medfield, and other burned towns | Pre-war church records; colony-level records; NEHGS compilations |
| **Incomplete early compliance** | Some towns didn't record consistently before the 1650s–1660s | Church records and colony court records |
| **Pre-1628 Massachusetts events** | No Massachusetts records exist before the colony's founding | English parish records for settlers' home parishes (Great Migration study cites English origins) |

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with the Great Migration study** if your ancestor arrived before 1643 — it's the single best-documented reference for Puritan settlers and will point you to every known source.

2. **Know your county for the event date.** Deeds and probate follow the county that existed *at the time*, not the modern one. The pre-1643 period means everything goes to colony-level records.

3. **Plymouth Colony is separate.** Don't assume FamilySearch's Massachusetts collections cover it fully. The published *Records of the Colony of New Plymouth* (12 vols., free on HathiTrust) is essential for Plymouth, Duxbury, Barnstable, and surrounding towns.

4. **Church membership lists establish household composition.** Admission dates can bracket births; discipline records sometimes note when a member moved to another town — a useful migration clue.

5. **Use associates and neighbors (the FAN club).** Puritan migration was chain-driven — families, neighbors, and congregation members traveled together. When you lose track of an ancestor, follow the people who appear alongside them.

6. **HathiTrust is free and rich.** The Shurtleff/Pulsifer colony record editions, Tanner–Corbin vital records series, and many local histories are fully searchable there at no cost.

7. **Further reading:** FamilySearch wiki articles [Massachusetts Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Massachusetts_Genealogy) and [Massachusetts Church Records](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Massachusetts_Church_Records) give additional county-level detail.

---

A few things to be aware of: The guide covers the whole colony rather than a specific county because in the early 1600s the county system didn't exist until 1643, and the core repositories (Massachusetts Archives, NEHGS, Congregational Library) serve the entire colonial period regardless of town. If you want to narrow to a specific county or town, I can produce a more focused guide.

Now compiling the guide from all gathered data.Here's your locality guide for Oklahoma's Land Run era. The key things to know:

---

## Oklahoma Territory — Land Run Era (1889–1907): What to Search

### Start with land records — they're uniquely rich here

Every Land Run participant filed a homestead application on April 22, 1889. Two complementary resources:

- **BLM General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov) — free, searchable by name. Shows the patent itself.
- **NARA Fort Worth** — the full homestead *case file*, often 20–100 pages, containing birth date, birthplace, citizenship status, neighbors' testimony, and a physical description of improvements. This is what you really want. Not online; must request from NARA.

FamilySearch also has **"Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907"** (234K records, indexed + images).

---

### The 1890 census is gone — here's how to bridge the gap

The 1890 federal census was destroyed in a 1921 fire. For the 1889–1900 window, use:

| Substitute | What it covers |
|---|---|
| Oklahoma Territorial Census, 1890 (fragments) | Logan and Oklahoma Counties; OHS, browse-only |
| County tax rolls from 1890 | Annual, locate family by county each year |
| Homestead prove-up newspaper notices | Names claimant + 2–4 witnesses (often kin) |
| Marriage records (FamilySearch, 1890–1995) | 456K records, indexed + images |
| Church records | Baptisms/burials substitute for missing civil vitals |

The **1900 federal census is your anchor** — it's the first full enumeration, asks parents' birthplaces, years married, and children born/living.

---

### Civil vital records don't start until 1908

Oklahoma didn't require statewide birth/death registration until October 1908 — a full year after statehood. For 1889–1907, go to church records, cemetery inscriptions, newspapers, and probate files.

---

### Oklahoma Territory vs. Indian Territory — a critical distinction

If your ancestor lived in eastern Oklahoma (Tahlequah, Muskogee, Tulsa area), they were in **Indian Territory** — a completely separate federal jurisdiction with its own records:

- **Dawes Commission Rolls (1898–1914)** — enrolled all Five Civilized Tribes members (Cherokee, Choctaw, Chickasaw, Creek, Seminole). FamilySearch collection has 567K records, indexed + images.
- **U.S. Court for the Indian Territory** records at NARA Fort Worth.
- Freedmen's Dawes Roll covers formerly enslaved people of the Tribes — relevant for African American families in eastern Oklahoma.

---

### Key repositories

| Where | What |
|---|---|
| **BLM GLO** (glorecords.blm.gov) — free online | Land patents |
| **FamilySearch** — free online | Land records, Indian rolls, marriages, church records |
| **Ancestry/Fold3** — subscription | Census, pension files, Dawes Rolls, draft registrations |
| **Chronicling America** (loc.gov) — free | Oklahoma territorial newspapers 1889–1922, full-text searchable |
| **Oklahoma Historical Society (OHS)**, Oklahoma City | Territorial census fragments, tax rolls, county histories, African American collection |
| **NARA Fort Worth** | Homestead case files, Indian Territory court records, Dawes Commission files |
| **County courthouses** | Marriage licenses (1890+), probate files, deeds |

---

### The prove-up notice trick

Federal law required homesteaders to *publish* a notice in a local paper naming themselves and two witnesses before receiving final title. The **Guthrie Daily Leader** (founded April 22, 1889 — the day of the run) is full of these. Search them on Chronicling America at `chroniclingamerica.loc.gov` — free, full-text. The witnesses named are almost always neighbors or relatives.

---
